findings.description: charge_order never checks the order's existing status before charging, so a retry, double-click, or concurrent call charges the customer's card twice for the same order (no idempotency guard, unlike refund which does check status).

After a successful gateway.charge, the UPDATE/commit can raise (DB locked, disk full) and the exception propagates with no catch or compensating reversal, leaving money taken but the order never marked paid (dual-write inconsistency).

charge_order sets status='paid' and tx_id but never writes paid_at, yet monthly_revenue_report filters strictly on strftime('%Y'/'%m', paid_at); nothing in scope sets paid_at, so paid orders will never appear in the revenue report.

monthly_revenue_report computes revenue_cents as raw unit_price*quantity (no discount, no tax) whereas charge_order charges compute_order_total (discount + tax applied), so reported revenue never reconciles with amounts actually charged.

refund only flips the DB status to 'refunded' and never calls the gateway to reverse the original charge, so the customer's money is never actually returned (refund-in-name-only); no reversal tx or refunded_at is recorded either.

No validation that discount_pct is within [0,100]; a value >100 makes (1 - discount/100) negative, producing negative line/subtotal/total and ultimately a non-positive amount_cents that the gateway silently rejects, and a value <0 over-charges.

Full card_number (PAN) and cvv are written to the application log in plaintext, a severe PCI-DSS violation (CVV must never be stored/logged post-auth); flagged in passing, defer remediation detail to critic-security.

compute_order_total returns subtotal*(1+TAX_RATE) without rounding, so fractional-cent tax propagates to the returned dollar total; only masked because charge_order re-rounds at line 73, but any other caller of this public method gets an unrounded currency value.
monthly_revenue_report issues one order_items query per order (N+1); could be a single JOIN/GROUP BY aggregate; flagged in passing, defer detail to critic-performance.

int(round(total*100)) uses Python's banker's rounding (round-half-to-even), which can differ from commercial round-half-up on exact half-cent boundaries, occasionally charging a cent less than expected.
